title: Chana Masala

ingredients.title: For the Chickpeas
items: 2 cups dried chickpeas (kabuli chana), soaked overnight
1 black tea bag or 2 black cardamom pods (optional – for deeper color)

ingredients.title: For the Masala Base
items: 3 tbsp oil or ghee (restaurant kitchens often use a mix)
2 medium tomatoes, puréed (or 1 cup tomato puree)

ingredients.title: Ground Spices
items: 1½ tsp red chili powder (adjust to taste)
1 tsp garam masala (preferably homemade or premium)
½ tsp amchur (dry mango powder) or a squeeze of lemon juice

instruction.title: Step 1: Cook the Chickpeas
steps: Drain the soaked chickpeas and rinse.
Pressure cook (or simmer) with 5 cups water, salt, and the tea bag/cardamom until tender (about 35–40 min in a pressure cooker, or 60–70 min stovetop). Discard tea bag. Reserve some cooking liquid.

instruction.title: Step 2: Prepare the Masala
steps: Heat oil/ghee in a heavy-bottomed pan.
Add cumin seeds and bay leaf; let them splutter.
Add onions, sauté until golden-brown (patience here gives restaurant depth).
Stir in ginger-garlic paste and green chilies; cook until raw aroma fades.
Add all powdered spices (except garam masala & amchur) – toast lightly in oil for aroma.
Add tomato purée; cook on medium heat until the masala thickens and oil separates (bhunao technique).

instruction.title: Step 3: Combine Chickpeas & Masala
steps: Add cooked chickpeas along with 1–1.5 cups of the reserved stock.
Simmer 20–25 minutes, mashing a few chickpeas with the back of the spoon to thicken the gravy.
Adjust salt, consistency should be medium-thick and glossy.
Finish with garam masala, amchur, and fresh coriander.

instruction.title: Step 4: Garnish & Serve
steps: Serve hot, topped with ginger juliennes, fresh coriander, and lemon wedges.
Pair with bhature, puri, jeera rice, or naan.

chefTips: Slow bhunao (roasting of masala): This is what gives body and richness. Never rush the onion-tomato base.
Tea bag trick: Gives chana a deep restaurant-style brown color.
Stock use: Always add chickpea cooking liquid for extra flavor instead of plain water.
Oil float: A slight layer of oil on top signals the masala is perfectly cooked.
Overnight flavor: Tastes even better the next day, as spices mature.
